other_observers: Glenn Walbek, Steve Stachowiak, Larry Arnold, Paul Differding, Todd Deininger, and Steve Larson.; found and identified by Glenn and myself
description: the bird was a medium sized dabbler, about the same size and the same shape as the adjacent American Wigeons. The bill was pale blue gray. I did not see the legs.  The head of the bird was brownish rufous, fairly even in color except for a tiny spot of iridescent green behind the eye and a pale, buffy colored crown. The color of the head was a bit less intensely rufous colored than that of a fully alternate bird; I believe this is because the bird was not entirely out of eclipse plumage.  The chest of the bird was pinkish buffy colored, cleanly separated from light gray flanks. The back was also light gray. There upper part of the flanks, just below the wings, has a little bit of rufous feathering, similar in color to the head. I believe this was the remnant of eclipse plumage. Rear flanks and lower belly was white; most visible in flight. The undertail coverts were black.   The flight feathers of the bird were very dark gray. The secondary coverts were white, creating a bold white patch visible in flight. The secondaries were iridescent green. The underwings were clean gray, with a slight whitening in the axillaries, but without any strong contrast between tones of gray.Behaviors: The bird was first found walking around on the mud of a small reservoir. It was in the company of a number of American Wigeon. Upon approach, the whole flock flew and remained in the air until out departure. Later in the day we refound the bird swimming on the reservoir.Call: none heardPlumage: molting from eclipse into alternate
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer
